title: The map archive in Antwerp
summary: Eighty-six drawers of historical maps — many of them wrong — line the basement of a museum in Antwerp. The archivist explains what wrong maps actually are.
body:
The Plantin-Moretus museum in Antwerp keeps the oldest printing house in the world. It is a UNESCO site. It has heavy wooden presses from the sixteenth century, ink-stained floors, and books that have not been opened in two hundred years. But the part I went to see was not the press room. It was the map archive in the basement.

The archive has eighty-six wooden drawers. Each drawer holds maps. The maps are from the years 1545 to about 1750. Many of them are wrong. Some of them are very wrong. There is a map of Africa that shows the continent ending sharply just south of the Sahara. There is a map of North America that includes a mountain range that does not exist. There is a map of the Netherlands that shows a forest in the middle of what is now the city of Rotterdam.

I was given gloves and shown to a table. I was allowed to look at three drawers a day. The archivist is a woman named Anika, who had worked there for twenty-two years. I asked her, on the first afternoon, what she had learned from looking at so many maps that turned out to be wrong.

She thought for a long time. She rarely answers quickly. Then she said: 'I have learned that a wrong map is not a lie. It is what someone honestly believed, and drew very carefully, using the best information they had. The cartographer is not the villain of the map. The cartographer is the witness.'

She paused. 'I have also learned to be gentler with the present. Our maps now look correct. They are correct in the same way the 1620 maps were correct in 1620. Some of what we draw today will be the mountain that does not exist, three hundred years from now. We will not be liars. We will be the same kind of witness.'

I went home from that visit and re-read a report I had written six months earlier about something I had felt sure of at the time. I noticed that the certainty was a kind of mapmaker's confidence. It was based on what I had then. It would have been irresponsible to draw the map without it. It would also be irresponsible, in five years, to imagine that the map was the territory.

The maps in Antwerp are not embarrassing. They are honest. They look out at us from behind their glass, with their phantom mountains and their cut-off continents, and they ask one quiet question of every visitor: what is on your map that no one will see for what it is, until you are long gone?

annotations.meaningVi: lãnh thổ / vùng đất thật ngoài đời

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for actions before another past event
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi kể chuyện trong quá khứ, đôi khi cần nói đến chuyện xảy ra trước thời điểm đang kể. B1 dùng quá khứ hoàn thành ('had + V3'). Trong bài: 'Anika, who had worked there for twenty-two years.' (tiếp tục công việc trước thời điểm 'I went') 'what she had learned from looking at so many maps' (đã học trước câu hỏi) 'a report I had written six months earlier' (viết trước lúc đọc lại) 'something I had felt sure of at the time' (cảm giác chắc chắn ở thời điểm trước). 'Had + V3' giúp tách biệt hai lớp thời gian trong quá khứ.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: When telling a past story, you sometimes need to refer to something that happened before the main past moment. Use past perfect ('had + past participle'). 'Anika, who had worked there for twenty-two years.' (already at the time of the visit) 'What she had learned from looking at so many maps.' (learned before being asked) 'A report I had written six months earlier.' (written before re-reading it). Past perfect separates two layers of past time.

translationVi:
Bảo tàng Plantin-Moretus ở Antwerp lưu giữ nhà in cổ nhất thế giới. Đây là di sản UNESCO. Có những máy in gỗ nặng từ thế kỷ 16, sàn gỗ loang mực, và những cuốn sách đã không được mở suốt hai trăm năm. Nhưng phần tôi đến xem không phải phòng in. Là kho bản đồ dưới tầng hầm.

Kho có tám mươi sáu ngăn kéo gỗ. Mỗi ngăn chứa bản đồ. Bản đồ từ những năm 1545 đến khoảng 1750. Nhiều cái sai. Vài cái rất sai. Có một tấm bản đồ châu Phi vẽ lục địa kết thúc đột ngột ngay phía nam Sahara. Có một bản đồ Bắc Mỹ thêm vào một dãy núi không hề tồn tại. Có một bản đồ Hà Lan vẽ một khu rừng ngay giữa nơi bây giờ là thành phố Rotterdam.

Tôi được phát găng tay và chỉ vào một cái bàn. Tôi được phép xem ba ngăn kéo một ngày. Thủ thư là một phụ nữ tên Anika, đã làm việc ở đây hai mươi hai năm. Buổi chiều đầu, tôi hỏi cô đã học được gì sau bao năm xem những tấm bản đồ hoá ra sai.

Cô suy nghĩ rất lâu. Cô hiếm khi trả lời nhanh. Rồi cô nói: 'Tôi đã học được rằng một tấm bản đồ sai không phải là một lời nói dối. Nó là cái ai đó đã thành tâm tin, và đã vẽ rất cẩn thận, bằng thông tin tốt nhất họ có. Người vẽ bản đồ không phải kẻ phản diện của bản đồ. Người vẽ bản đồ là nhân chứng.'

Cô dừng lại. 'Tôi cũng đã học để dịu dàng hơn với hiện tại. Bản đồ của chúng ta bây giờ trông đúng. Chúng đúng theo cùng kiểu mà những bản đồ 1620 từng đúng vào năm 1620. Một phần những gì chúng ta vẽ hôm nay sẽ là cái dãy núi không tồn tại của ba trăm năm sau. Chúng ta sẽ không phải kẻ nói dối. Chúng ta sẽ là cùng một loại nhân chứng đó.'

Về nhà sau chuyến đi, tôi đọc lại một bản báo cáo tôi đã viết sáu tháng trước, về một chuyện tôi đã thấy chắc chắn lúc đó. Tôi nhận ra cái chắc chắn ấy là một loại tự tin của người vẽ bản đồ. Nó dựa trên những gì tôi có khi đó. Sẽ là vô trách nhiệm nếu vẽ bản đồ mà không có nó. Nhưng cũng sẽ vô trách nhiệm, sau năm năm nữa, nếu tưởng rằng bản đồ chính là lãnh thổ.

Những bản đồ ở Antwerp không phải để xấu hổ. Chúng trung thực. Chúng nhìn ra chúng ta từ phía sau lớp kính, với những dãy núi ma và những lục địa bị cắt, và chúng hỏi mỗi du khách một câu lặng lẽ: trên bản đồ của bạn có gì mà người ta sẽ chỉ nhận ra khi bạn đã không còn?

options: A UNESCO site that keeps the oldest printing house in the world
A library of new books only
questions.explanation: 'The Plantin-Moretus museum in Antwerp keeps the oldest printing house in the world. It is a UNESCO site.'

questions.prompt: How many wooden drawers does the map archive have, and from what years?
options: Eighty-six drawers, with maps from 1545 to about 1750
Eight hundred drawers, from medieval times
questions.explanation: 'The archive has eighty-six wooden drawers… The maps are from the years 1545 to about 1750.'

questions.prompt: Which of these are described as wrong on old maps?
options: Africa shown as ending sharply south of the Sahara; a non-existent mountain range in North America; a forest where Rotterdam now stands
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 lists the three specific errors.

questions.prompt: How does Anika answer 'what she had learned from looking at so many maps that turned out to be wrong'?
options: A wrong map is not a lie — it is what someone honestly believed, drawn carefully with the best information they had. The cartographer is the witness.
Modern technology is better than old technology.
To be gentler with the present — our current maps will look wrong from three hundred years away, and we will be the same kind of witness, not liars.

questions.prompt: What does the writer do after the visit?
options: She re-reads a report she had written six months earlier and notices that her certainty was 'a mapmaker's confidence.'
She refuses to write any more reports.
questions.explanation: 'I went home from that visit and re-read a report I had written six months earlier… I noticed that the certainty was a kind of mapmaker's confidence.'

questions.prompt: What is 'the map is the territory' a warning against?
options: Confusing your current model (the map) with the underlying reality (the territory) — especially across time.
questions.explanation: 'It would also be irresponsible, in five years, to imagine that the map was the territory.'

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe the old maps' question to visitors?
options: 'What is on your map that no one will see for what it is, until you are long gone?'
questions.explanation: The final line of the passage.

questions.prompt: What is the writer NOT claiming?
options: That maps from 1545–1750 were all dishonest.
That cartographers worked carefully with the best information they had.
That present-day certainty might look wrong from a future vantage point.
That wrong maps deserve to be called honest.
questions.explanation: Anika and the writer explicitly say a wrong map is not a lie.
questions.hint: Look for the option that says the maps were dishonest.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer describe Anika's manner as 'rarely answers quickly'?
options: Her answers come from a long, slow habit of looking at carefully drawn wrong things — they take time to form.
questions.explanation: The slowness fits the work — maps from centuries of careful effort.
questions.hint: Notice when Anika pauses, and the kind of work she does.

questions.prompt: What does 'mapmaker's confidence' mean in the writer's report?
options: The honest confidence that comes from drawing a careful conclusion with the best information available at the time
questions.explanation: She links it explicitly to the cartographer's honest careful work.
questions.hint: Connect it back to Anika's definition of a wrong map.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Being wrong with care is not the same as being dishonest — and accepting this should make us gentler with both the past and our own present certainties.
All museums should have map archives.
Antwerp is the best city in Belgium.
questions.explanation: The whole essay reframes 'wrong' as 'honest witness within available information.'
questions.hint: Notice the bridge from the historical maps to the writer's own report.

It has heavy wooden presses from the sixteenth century, ink-stained floors, and books that have not been opened in two hundred years. But the part I went to see was not the press room. It was the map archive in the {{b1}}.

There is a map of the Netherlands that shows a forest in the middle of what is now the city of Rotterdam.

She thought for a long time. She rarely answers quickly. Then she said: 'I have learned that a wrong map is not a {{b5}}. It is what someone honestly believed, and drew very carefully, using the best information they had. The cartographer is not the villain of the map. The cartographer is the {{b6}}.'

She paused. 'I have also learned to be gentler with the {{b7}}. Our maps now look correct. They are correct in the same way the 1620 maps were correct in 1620. Some of what we draw today will be the mountain that does not exist, three hundred years from now. We will not be liars. We will be the same kind of witness.'

I went home from that visit and re-read a report I had written six months earlier about something I had felt sure of at the time. I noticed that the certainty was a kind of mapmaker's {{b8}}. It was based on what I had then. It would have been irresponsible to draw the map without it. It would also be irresponsible, in five years, to imagine that the map was the {{b9}}.

The maps in Antwerp are not embarrassing. They are honest. They look out at us from behind their glass, with their {{b10}} mountains and their cut-off continents, and they ask one quiet question of every visitor: what is on your map that no one will see for what it is, until you are long {{b11}}?

blanks.explanation: The map archive is in the basement.
A mountain range that does not exist.
A wrong map is not a lie.
Don't confuse the map with the territory.
Phantom mountains — mountains that don't exist.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Anika says she has learned to be 'gentler with the present' because we are the same kind of witness as the 1620 mapmakers. What is something you believe firmly today that you suspect — honestly — future generations may find as quaint as a forest in the middle of Rotterdam? Why do you still draw it on your map?
